speaker: interviewer
text: Number three-- I'm speaking with Mr. George Eisenberg. Mr. Eisenberg, you were just explaining to me that as winter approached in the forest, you and your friends build a bunker, and that quite a few more people joined you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any scares of Germans searching for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to search every day and every night. They used to go out with dogs to search. But you know, it's a funny thing. If a person is in a situation like this, I don't know if I could do this again today. But you learn how to survive. We found out that a dog can not sniff anybody out if there was pine wood. [INAUDIBLE] pine, the dog cannot sniff. So the forest where we were-- were mostly like pine woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they used to go out, we could hear their dogs barking and everything. They used to come down even with planes to search for us. But they killed a lot of-- I mean, the escaped, there were a lot of people who-- who escaped from other camp. But were [INAUDIBLE] also. But for some reason, you know, we managed to hide away from them. Did you, during this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now-- wait. I'll tell you. Now, the problem was now, it comes in the winter time. Right, like I told you, we used to go out-- in the summertime, we used to go out and to gather food. What are you doing in the wintertime? How do you go out from your bunker?

speaker: interviewer
text: We had snows in there, not like in here. We had snow, two, three feet, of snow, snow banks [INAUDIBLE]. How do you go out from a place to eliminate the footsteps? Because the easiest way to get somebody in the wintertime, that's the easiest way. You just follow the track from the footsteps. So we came-- an [? improvation ?] we used to do. Take a log from a pine wood. And a pine wood has like, like needles. And attach it, tie it on, to your back. As you walk away, this one, it just covers back the snow. There's no signs of footsteps. And that's how we did it. Didn't that make a depression in the snow, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I just told you. It did not make an impression, because the impression you had, the pine needles swept it away. Like you're-- like on this wintertime, you go out there. There is a snow. If you take a broom, and you sweep it away, it leaves no impression. It just leaves the impression from the broomsticks or whatever, from the needle. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have yourselves organized within this bunker with special jobs for special people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had no special jobs. Everybody did-- well, everybody did whatever they could do. I mean, there was nothing much to do. There was no books to read. Just to eat, to sleep, and then, that's-- that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And look.

speaker: interviewer
text: --get any information from the outside world at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing at all, nothing at all-- we had no contact with anybody. We were afraid to walk out, to make contact with anybody. You-- you had no idea what is going on. And war's going on in, and then that's it, until we were liberated from the Russians, which was in 1944. I was liberated in 1944 from the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: What month was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in February, the 28th. I think it was February 28. [SNIFFS] So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall to me how that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: The way it happened-- OK, we went out. We used to go-- a few weeks before, there was some movement in the forest. We could hear a lot of shooting. And at that time, the Russians had these Katyushas, the Russian Katyusha. We could see them go right over our heads. And the Germans were also shooting with their-- I think they were telling us they were shooting with V-2 rockets. And this was going on for quite a long time. They destroyed a lot towns, a lot of villages. So, and one night-- it was before we were liberated maybe, a night before, we were-- in that time, we already made contact with another few people in the-- in the different bunkers, you know, and different [INAUDIBLE]. And then we decided to go out. It was, I remember, like today, it was a very cold night. And we all decided to go out to get us some food. Because in that time, we already-- there was a lot of killed animals, you know, pigs, cows, horses, and everything. So we knew already that something was going on. So finally, after the shooting stopped, we decide we'll go out and look for food. And we came out to this small hamlet, to this village. And there was only one house left in there. So we went in there. And this guy, all of a sudden, the Ukrainians start to become friendly. But then they saw [INAUDIBLE]. And the guy told us. He said, listen. Try to stay alive because the Russians are coming in here any minute, any day.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so while we were there, you know, we seen-- we-- we gathered some food. We get some-- some meat and some other food. And we put them in sacks, and we start to go out, going back to our place to our forest bunker. What we did not know was the Russian patrol, the forest-- how do you call this? The first-- Reconnaissance?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it, the first reconnaissance, the reconnaissance were coming in. But we don't know if it's the Germans or Russians. So we started on the way and hided. And we came back. We came back to our place. Except here goes sort of my friend-- this friend of mine, this [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE]. He was the one who was left behind. For some reason, he didn't follow us. He left behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sure enough, the Russians caught him. He didn't know if it was Russians or Germans. So they start to investigate him. They said, where are you coming from or what? He says he's a Jew. He's hiding, [INAUDIBLE] and this, and this, he said. So where are the other Jews? They asked.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I don't know. There's no other Jews. You know, we were trained [INAUDIBLE] to not to give away one another. He said, there are no other Jews. He says I'm the only one. I'm the only survivor. I run in there. I'm hungry. I want to get some food, and that's all of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said to him, OK, you could go. And they let him go. Sure enough, what he did not know was that they were following him. And sure enough, they found him the next day. And they came in to our bunker.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came in. And there was a Jewish officer who came up. And he said, listen. Don't be afraid. He says, you're safe now. You'll be OK. [INAUDIBLE] the Russian army-- the Russian army is going to be here tonight. And you all going to go along with us. You're liberated. Ah, we didn't believe it. Sure enough, in the night time, we could hear-- you know, I-- and [INAUDIBLE], we were mostly-- we were talking our language was mostly Ukrainian. Ukrainian to Russian is similar. And beside, when I went to school, it was a Russian school. So we knew the language was good, and we heard the Russian language. Well, we found out later, at that time was also-- the Polish underground was active, you know. There was a [INAUDIBLE] army, or whatever kind of thing, yeah. But we found out. They told us, you know, there were some other people that were elderly people. We were still so young, but there were people who were elder-- or older than us. And they joined-- the-- the Polish underground told them that they're going to take him in the underground, and the partisans. Well, we found out later, after a few weeks, was-- the next day, that we wouldn't be liberated from the Russians. There was an order to shoot us all, to get rid of all the Jews [INAUDIBLE] in the area. So sure enough, in the night time, the Russians came along. And we went along with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, on that time, particularly me and Michael, we were about 10. And that time, we were all together, we were about me, [INAUDIBLE], we were about five, six together, where we stuck together. And there was other people also.

speaker: interviewer
text: So also, one time, as we were marching, the Germans were coming in. The Germans were trying to fight the Russians, you know. They were coming in by planes, and they were bombing the Russian army. So you could see that people get killed. Horses get killed. And-- but it was no remorse, nothing. It was like, like that's the way it's supposed to be. So we marching along. Did you take any of the Russian uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I took any of the Russian uniforms? No, I didn't have to take [INAUDIBLE]. Yeah, I had a-- yeah, I had a Russian uniform. But this was later on. What happened then, also one incident, which I'm-- you totally-- you were liberated. You were really free. But now, you don't know what tomorrow brings. We coming in to one little village. It's called-- I remember the name-- it was called [NON-ENGLISH]. And we going in. The Russian command was right across the street. And we start to sleep. People going to take bed. We're going to sleep in this house where there's a farmer, a Ukrainian. We had some food in the night time. You know, and the guy put-- you know, we had no mattresses sort of, but just straw on the floor. So we were laying down, falling asleep. In the meantime, some of the Russian soldiers, they get drunk. And they starting to go in, and they want to have a good time. They're looking for women. And they're looking for more vodka. Sure enough, two Russians coming in to the place where we sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're sleeping on the floor. And they're waking us up. And they say, who are you? Ah, we tell them. We-- no, no, no good, no good, you German spies. We're going to shoot you. What do you mean you're going to shoot me? For what?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it. Let's go outside. We're going to line you up. We're going to shoot you, you German spies. They take-- I was hiding in there. I was looking in there. And they took the other three people-- [INAUDIBLE], with [INAUDIBLE], with Parness, [INAUDIBLE] Parness. I remember that's the name. They took them outside. They lined them up against the wall. And they Were turning their machine guns. They were going to shoot. When I saw this, I jumped out. And I went to-- to the headquarters, the Russian headquarters. It was like across the street. So I went in there. And I talked to the guy who was in charge.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, listen. We Jews, we survived the concentration camp. Now, these two guys, these two soldiers are going to shoot us. He said, what are you talking about? I say, you better hurry up and go out.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, sure enough, he goes out. And these guys having already their rifles positioned. So they grabbed them. They says, OK, away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so we went-- so we decided it's not a good idea to go along with them. So we separated, and we went to [INAUDIBLE]. And we went and saw for a few days, until we come to another areas. And after a few weeks, we reached-- we came to-- where did we go after that? [NON-ENGLISH], no, we came to a different places.

speaker: interviewee
text: Although we came to our town. Not actually, we didn't want to go back to our town. But we came to a nearby town. And we were there for a while. And then after they start of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had all this area been occupied by Russians by now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, as they go-- as they were going along, the Russians occupied. Then we went along. Whichever was occupied area, we were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, we wound up in a place. It was called [NON-ENGLISH]. And there, we already had-- we establish. We had-- we met some other Jews, because [NON-ENGLISH] was a big city.

speaker: interviewee
text: We met some other Jews. And we were staying there. Of course, then more people came along. [COUGHS] And the Russians started to take-- even we were escaping, even we were survivors from camp, from concentration camps, they needed people in the army, in the Russian army because the war was still going on. That was in '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the people who were older, who were in their age, as military age, they took them away. And a lot of them got killed also in the Russian front, after they survived this concentration camp. And so they got killed later on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, in [NON-ENGLISH], I found out-- I found a cousin of mine who survived. His name is [? Josef. ?] And he met me. He was with the Russian army. He was also-- he's a concentration camp survivor. And he-- he was in a bad camp. He was in [NON-ENGLISH] That was the worst one in [NON-ENGLISH]. For-- lucky enough for him, he survived. And he's a very intelligent person. And he had a good [INAUDIBLE]. He was in charge of the Russian army. On this particular [INAUDIBLE] he was in charge of to supply the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I met him so we already were OK. So he took me. So we went over there, and I lived with him. And it was going on OK. Until later on, we decided to go back to his hometown which is Lemberg, Lvov.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came in there, and and Lvov, already, There was already and by the end of '44 and the beginning of '44. So [COUGHS] there were a lot of Jews in there. And you know, so then people start talking about going. We still have to go, because nobody wanted to live in there. Like I would never want to go back to Sasow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we decide. And we still we didn't know what was going to happen, so we still went. And there was still the war going on. So from Lemberg we went to Przemysl. And by the way, also in Lemberg, this cousin of mine-- actually, I could say he was the first one who, actually, was one of the first ones, who started the [NON-ENGLISH]. Which [NON-ENGLISH] means so-- flight. So being he was in charge, he was a big deal. So like in Russia, to go from one place to another, you need a-- it's not called a [NON-ENGLISH], but it's called a [NON-ENGLISH]. Like a ID-- you cannot go from one place to another without having any kind of documents papers. So he is the one who used to sign all these papers. And that was started out in [NON-ENGLISH] with a few of the Jewish people who were signers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said that we have to start to try to go to get to Israel. And there, one time, some people tried to smuggle the border between Poland and Romania. And they got caught. Two or three of them got killed. And they found the ID which my cousin signed, that he's the one who gave them. At that time, I was in Lemberg, and we lived in Lvov also. So I told him that they were looking for him. So I came down. And I told him that we have to run from this [INAUDIBLE]. So we got together. And we ran.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so where did we go? We went to Przemysl. Przemysl is a big city in Poland. And you was asking before if I wore a Russian uniform. Then what happened on that time, he's the one who made papers for me and for himself that we are Russian intelligence, like [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was having-- I was wearing a Russian uniform. And he was wearing a Russian uniform. And we can came to Przemysl. We came to Przemysl, but another story. We came to Przemysl, and there was still the war going on. And they had a curfew. You were only allowed to, like, till 6 o'clock in the evening. Just like 6:00 in the evening, you were not allowed to be outside. [BACKGROUND NOISE] For some reason, I went once. I went-- I was going to visit a friend of mine. And I was on my way home. And it was early curfew hour. I was not supposed to be there. But being I was like, I had a Russian uniform, I thought I'm a big shot. Didn't work that way. Two Russian soldiers approached me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said to me, what are you doing in here? I said, I'm going home. The Russians were always looking for clocks, for watches. That was the biggest piece of jewelry for them is a watch. Which in Russian is called [RUSSIAN]. So I had a watch on my hand. It was, I don't know, a cheap watch at all. They said they wanted the watch. I said, well, I don't want to give you the watch. They said, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have to take you down to the headquarters. OK. So they put their rifles with bayonets. They caught a big fish. They taking me to the headquarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they take me? [COUGHS] I said, go ahead. Take me to the-- to your commander. I had papers that I work with the Russian army. And they said, OK, we're going to [INAUDIBLE]. To take me in there, what did they do? They put me in a barrack together with all these Ukrainian [INAUDIBLE]. The [INAUDIBLE], they were the ones who were killing Jews and Russians. They were the-- like the underground, the Ukrainians. They worked together with the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: What the Russians did to them, they-- when they caught them, is to put them in trucks and bring them right out to the front without any training like chopped meat. They were the first one in the line to go in the [INAUDIBLE]. So I've been there the whole night. There's no-- there's only standing only. You cannot sit down. There was-- the one was so packed, thousands, or hundreds, of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the morning, it's still dark. They coming in. They say, everybody out. Everybody out. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was where-- the jail, where the main jail was also in Przemysl. So they take the-- I seen the big trucks, army trucks. And they take these guys. They put them up in the truck. And they're putting them in there. I'm looking on. I said, to myself well, if I ever go in this truck, I'll never be alive. Better stay away [INAUDIBLE]. Well, OK, so in the meantime, I'm looking. And the guy who's in charge who's giving all this-- this commands, he said, it seems to me like a Jewish officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, OK. I jump out from the line where I am. And the soldiers all looked at me. He said, where are you going? I said, I want to see the commander. So I go over to this man, who I think he was a captain. And I [INAUDIBLE]. I said, listen. I was good in Russia. I says, listen, I'm a Jewish survivor. I survived the concentration camp. I said, now you sending me with all these [INAUDIBLE] you're going to send me to? He's looking at me. He says, what are you doing in here? I said, I don't know. I said these two guys, they caught me last night. He said, where are your papers? I said, they have my papers. So he calls over these guys. He said, hey, wait a minute. Where are the papers from this guy?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's wearing a Russian uniform. Why did you-- Well, we didn't know with this. He said, let me see his papers. He said, well, we don't have them. He said, well, he said, I'll give you five minutes. Within five minutes, you come back with his papers. And to me, he says, you stay right in here. Don't move. Don't go nowhere. OK, sure enough, they come back with the papers. The guy starts screaming at them. He says-- he said, I'm going to court martial you. You had no right to arrest this boy-- this man. He said, you had no right at all. He said, get out of my sight. And I'm staying with him. He says to me. Listen, stay here. Don't move because I'll take care of you. Sure enough, after this, after a couple hours later, he said you come along with me. He takes me up to his place where he lived. And he gives me some food. And he's telling me. He said, listen. He says, I'm a Jew. He says, the Germans killed my whole family, my wife, my kids, my family. He says, I made an oath to God. I swore to God if I come into Germany, he says, I'm not going to have no mercy on anybody. Whoever I find, and how long I have the time, I'm going to kill them like they killed my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, he said, now, with you-- he said, you here alone? I said I was afraid to come. I said, no, I have a cousin of mine. Because my cousin was already of the age to be inducted the army. I said I have a cousin of mine.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says-- he said, OK. He said, listen to me. I'm going to give you a paper. You can move all over Przemysl. You can go wherever you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: For you, for your cousin, and what I want you to do, he says, I want you to get away from here. Because we are not sure if we are going to stay in here. I said, where am I going? He said, well-- he said, I'm going to have some trucks going toward Romania and Hungary. Because that was liberated much before. So he says, I'm going to send you in there. I said, OK. So sure, we were there in Przemysl a couple of weeks. Then I used to go up to him. He used to give us all the food and whatever we needed, and clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one time, he came up to me, and he says, listen. Tomorrow, I'm having a truck. An army truck is going to Kosow, which was near the border from Romania. He said, you go in this truck. I give you a paper. He says, you'll be OK though. OK. So that's what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got together with my cousin. And sure enough, we went with this truck. And we came-- came to Kosow. In Kosow on that time, there was also some Jewish people in there who survived. And they were organizing that-- and so we came into Kosow. I have to interrupt for a break.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
